1:
"There is therefore now no condemnation to them which are in Christ Jesus, who walk not after the flesh, but after the Spirit." The witnesses note the great declaration of no condemnation. Gill notes the apostle, having discoursed of the believer's inward struggle (Romans 7), now proceeds to the solid ground of spiritual peace and joy, arising from justification and adoption, the purposes of God, and the unchangeable love of God. The witnesses note Paul declaring that there is now no condemnation to those who are in Christ Jesus — the triumphant conclusion of the doctrine of justification; the believer, united to Christ and justified by his righteousness, freed forever from condemnation; the walking after the Spirit the mark, not the ground, of the freedom. The witnesses note no condemnation. The witnesses note in Christ Jesus.

The witnesses note no condemnation to those in Christ Jesus — the believer freed forever from condemnation by union with Christ.

So the witnesses make the verse the great declaration of no condemnation. The lesson the expositors draw is the believer's complete freedom from condemnation, grounded in union with Christ and his righteousness. The witnesses note the conclusion: "there is therefore now no condemnation" — Gill notes the apostle, having discoursed of the inward struggle (Romans 7), now proceeds to the solid ground of peace; the triumphant "therefore"; the conclusion of the doctrine of justification; the no condemnation. The expositors observe the ground — in Christ Jesus: "to them which are in Christ Jesus" — the freedom from condemnation belongs to those in Christ; the union with Christ the ground; the being in him; the believer in Christ free; "in Christ Jesus." The witnesses press the completeness of the freedom: "no condemnation" — none at all, now and forever; the believer, justified by Christ's righteousness, has no condemnation; the complete acquittal; the freedom entire; the condemnation removed; the believer secure. The expositors draw the lesson on the ground in union and justification: the no condemnation rests on union with Christ and his imputed righteousness — the believer in Christ shares his righteousness, bears no condemnation; the ground in Christ, not in self; the justification securing the freedom; the union the ground. The witnesses observe the walking after the Spirit: "who walk not after the flesh, but after the Spirit" — the walking after the Spirit is the mark, the evidence, of those in Christ — not the ground of the freedom (which is Christ's righteousness), but the description of the freed; the mark of the justified; the evidence, not the cause; the walk that follows the grace. The expositors note the distinction of mark and ground: the freedom is grounded in Christ; the walk after the Spirit marks those who are in him; the ground and the evidence; not justified by the walk, but the walk evidencing the justified; the distinction maintained. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the no condemnation, the in Christ Jesus, and the walk after the Spirit. The verse thus records no condemnation to those in Christ Jesus — teaching the believer's complete freedom from condemnation, grounded in union with Christ and his righteousness.
2:
"For the law of the Spirit of life in Christ Jesus hath made me free from the law of sin and death." The witnesses note the freedom by the law of the Spirit of life. Gill notes the words are of difficult interpretation: "the law of the Spirit of life in Christ Jesus" may mean the gospel, or the principle of grace and life wrought by the Spirit; "the law of sin and death" the law of Moses (which condemns) or the principle of indwelling sin. The witnesses note Paul giving the reason for the no condemnation — the law (governing principle) of the Spirit of life in Christ Jesus has freed the believer from the law of sin and death; the new principle of life by the Spirit delivering from the dominion of sin and the condemnation of the law; the liberating power of the Spirit's life. The witnesses note the law of the Spirit of life. The witnesses note freedom from sin and death.

The witnesses note the law of the Spirit of life freeing the believer from the law of sin and death — the liberating power of the Spirit.

So the witnesses make the verse the freedom by the law of the Spirit of life. The lesson the expositors draw is the new principle of life by the Spirit delivering from the dominion of sin and the condemnation of the law. The witnesses note the freeing power: "the law of the Spirit of life in Christ Jesus hath made me free" — the law (governing principle, ruling power) of the Spirit of life in Christ has freed the believer; the liberating principle; the Spirit's life; the freedom wrought. The expositors weigh the difficult terms that Gill marks: "the law of the Spirit of life in Christ Jesus" may mean the gospel (declaring deliverance), or the principle of grace and life wrought by the Spirit; "the law of sin and death" the law of Moses (which condemns) or the principle of indwelling sin; the terms variously read. The witnesses present the chief sense: the dominant reading takes "the law of the Spirit of life" as the new governing principle of spiritual life imparted by the Spirit (the reign of grace and life in Christ), and "the law of sin and death" as the ruling power of indwelling sin (which works death); the Spirit's life delivering from sin's dominion; the new principle overcoming the old. The expositors draw the lesson on the liberating power of the Spirit: the Spirit's life in the believer is a freeing power — delivering from the bondage of sin and the condemnation that sin brings; the Spirit's life the liberty; the new principle conquering the old; "where the Spirit of the Lord is, there is liberty" (2 Corinthians 3:17); the freedom by the Spirit. The witnesses observe the connection with v.1: the no condemnation (v.1) is grounded in this freedom — the Spirit's life delivering from the law of sin and death; the freedom the reason for the no condemnation; the Spirit's work securing the liberty; the ground of the freedom. The expositors note the two laws: the law of the Spirit of life (the new principle, grace, the Spirit) and the law of sin and death (the old principle, sin, condemnation); the two ruling powers; the conflict; the Spirit's life prevailing; the new master delivering from the old. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the law of the Spirit of life, the freedom from sin and death, and the liberating power of the Spirit. The verse thus records the law of the Spirit of life freeing the believer from the law of sin and death — teaching the new principle of life by the Spirit delivering from the dominion of sin and the condemnation of the law.
3:
"For what the law could not do, in that it was weak through the flesh, God sending his own Son in the likeness of sinful flesh, and for sin, condemned sin in the flesh:" The witnesses note God doing what the law could not — condemning sin in the flesh of his Son. Gill notes the law could not justify or give life, being weakened not in itself but through the flesh (the corrupt nature that cannot fulfil it); what the law could not do, God did. The witnesses note Paul declaring that what the law could not do (weak through the flesh), God did — sending his own Son in the likeness of sinful flesh, and as a sacrifice for sin, condemned sin in the flesh; the incarnation and the atonement, God's Son taking real flesh (yet sinless), and bearing the condemnation of sin in his body, so that sin is condemned and the sinner freed. The witnesses note the law weak through the flesh. The witnesses note sin condemned in the flesh.

The witnesses note God doing what the law could not — sending his Son in sinful-likeness flesh and condemning sin in the flesh.

So the witnesses make the verse God condemning sin in the flesh of his Son. The lesson the expositors draw is the incarnation and atonement — God's Son taking real flesh, bearing the condemnation of sin, so that sin is condemned and the sinner freed. The witnesses note the law's impotence: "what the law could not do, in that it was weak through the flesh" — Gill notes the law could not justify or give life, being weakened not in itself but through the flesh (the corrupt nature that cannot fulfil it); the law's impotence; the weakness through the flesh; the law unable to save. The expositors observe God's act: "God sending his own Son in the likeness of sinful flesh, and for sin" — God did what the law could not — sending his own Son, in the likeness of sinful flesh, and as a sacrifice for sin; the incarnation; the Son sent; the sacrifice for sin; God's act. The witnesses press "the likeness of sinful flesh": the Son came "in the likeness of sinful flesh" — true, real flesh (a real human nature), yet without sin; the likeness of sinful flesh, not sinful flesh itself; the real incarnation, the sinless Christ; "in all points tempted... yet without sin" (Hebrews 4:15); the careful phrase guarding both the reality and the sinlessness. The expositors draw the lesson on condemning sin in the flesh: "condemned sin in the flesh" — God condemned sin in the flesh of Christ — executing the judgment due to sin upon the Son, in his body, as the sin-bearer; the sin condemned; the judgment borne; the atonement; the sin punished in Christ so the believer is freed; the condemnation laid on him. The witnesses observe the substitution: the sin was condemned in Christ's flesh — he bearing the penalty of sin in his body, as the substitute; the substitution; the sin-bearer; the condemnation borne by Christ; the believer's sin condemned in him. The expositors note the careful "for sin": "and for sin" — as a sacrifice for sin (the phrase used in the Greek Old Testament for the sin-offering); the sacrifice; the sin-offering; Christ the offering for sin; the atonement. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the law weak through the flesh, the Son in sinful-likeness flesh, and sin condemned in the flesh. The verse thus records God condemning sin in the flesh of his Son — teaching the incarnation and atonement, God's Son bearing the condemnation of sin so that sin is condemned and the sinner freed.
4:
"That the righteousness of the law might be fulfilled in us, who walk not after the flesh, but after the Spirit." The witnesses note the righteousness of the law fulfilled in believers. Gill notes by "the righteousness of the law" is meant the moral law's requirement — holiness of nature, righteousness of life; the active righteousness, obedience to the law's precepts; and by Christ's obedience to it we are made righteous. The witnesses note Paul declaring the purpose of the atonement — that the righteousness of the law might be fulfilled in believers, who walk after the Spirit; the law's righteous requirement met, chiefly by Christ's obedience imputed (fulfilled "in us" as our representative), and evidenced by the Spirit-led walk; the law honoured and the believer made righteous. The witnesses note the righteousness of the law fulfilled. The witnesses note the Spirit-led walk.

The witnesses note the law's righteousness fulfilled in believers — chiefly by Christ's imputed obedience, evidenced by the Spirit-led walk.

So the witnesses make the verse the righteousness of the law fulfilled in believers. The lesson the expositors draw is that the law's righteous requirement is met — chiefly by Christ's obedience imputed, and evidenced by the Spirit-led walk. The witnesses note the purpose: "that the righteousness of the law might be fulfilled in us" — the purpose of the atonement (v.3) was that the law's righteousness might be fulfilled in believers; Gill notes "the righteousness of the law" means the moral law's requirement (holiness, righteousness of life); the law's demand met; the righteousness fulfilled. The expositors weigh how it is fulfilled "in us": the chief sense is that the law's righteousness is fulfilled in believers by Christ's obedience imputed — Christ, as their representative, perfectly kept the law, and his righteousness is reckoned to them, so the law's demand is met "in us" (in our Head); the imputed obedience; the law fulfilled in Christ for us. The witnesses present the joined evidence in the walk: the fulfilment is also evidenced by the Spirit-led walk — believers, made righteous by Christ, walk after the Spirit, the law's righteousness now being worked out (imperfectly, but really) in their lives; the imputed righteousness and the Spirit-wrought obedience; the law fulfilled for us and increasingly in us. The expositors draw the lesson on the law honoured: the atonement honoured the law — Christ fulfilling its righteousness, the believer made righteous; the law not set aside but established (Romans 3:31); the law's demand met in Christ; the law honoured by the gospel. The witnesses observe the walk after the Spirit: "who walk not after the flesh, but after the Spirit" — the believers walk after the Spirit; the mark of the justified; the Spirit-led life; the walk evidencing the fulfilment; the new obedience. The expositors note the distinction of imputation and sanctification: the law's righteousness is fulfilled in us by imputation (Christ's obedience reckoned) and worked out by sanctification (the Spirit-led walk); the two; the ground and the evidence; the imputed and the imparted; the full fulfilment. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the righteousness of the law fulfilled, the Spirit-led walk, and the law honoured. The verse thus records the law's righteousness fulfilled in believers — teaching that the law's requirement is met chiefly by Christ's imputed obedience, evidenced by the Spirit-led walk.
5:
"For they that are after the flesh do mind the things of the flesh; but they that are after the Spirit the things of the Spirit." The witnesses note the two classes and their two minds. Gill notes by "flesh" is meant the corruption of nature; to be "after the flesh" is not merely to have flesh (the best saints have it), but to be governed by it — those who have nothing but corrupt nature, in whom it is the ruling principle. The witnesses note Paul distinguishing the two classes — those after the flesh mind the things of the flesh, those after the Spirit the things of the Spirit; the governing principle determining the bent of the mind, the affections and pursuits following the ruling nature; the two classes marked by what they mind. The witnesses note the two classes. The witnesses note minding the things of flesh or Spirit.

The witnesses note the two classes — the fleshly mind the things of the flesh, the spiritual the things of the Spirit; the ruling principle determining the bent.

So the witnesses make the verse the two classes and their two minds. The lesson the expositors draw is that the governing principle determines the bent of the mind, the affections and pursuits following the ruling nature. The witnesses note the fleshly: "they that are after the flesh do mind the things of the flesh" — Gill notes "after the flesh" is to be governed by the corrupt nature; the fleshly mind the things of the flesh — the carnal pursuits, the sensual, the worldly; the bent of the unregenerate; the carnal minding. The expositors observe the spiritual: "but they that are after the Spirit the things of the Spirit" — those after the Spirit (governed by the Spirit) mind the things of the Spirit — the spiritual, the heavenly, the things of God; the bent of the regenerate; the spiritual minding; the new pursuits. The witnesses press the governing principle: the ruling nature determines the minding — the flesh-governed mind the fleshly things, the Spirit-governed mind the spiritual things; the governing principle the key; the bent following the ruler; the mind shaped by the master. The expositors draw the lesson on the affections following the nature: the affections, thoughts, and pursuits follow the governing nature — the carnal heart loves the carnal, the spiritual heart the spiritual; the affections following the nature; the bent of the heart; "where your treasure is, there will your heart be also" (Matthew 6:21); the minding revealing the man. The witnesses observe the distinction Gill marks: to have flesh and to be after the flesh differ — the best saints have flesh in them, but are not after it (governed by it); the regenerate have the flesh, but the Spirit rules; the distinction; the having and the being-after; the flesh present but not ruling. The expositors note the test of the minding: the minding is the test of the class — what a man minds (habitually loves, pursues, sets his heart on) reveals whether he is after the flesh or the Spirit; the test of the bent; the minding the evidence; the heart's bent the mark. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the two classes, the minding of flesh or Spirit, and the governing principle. The verse thus records the two classes and their two minds — teaching that the governing principle determines the bent of the mind, the affections following the ruling nature.
6:
"For to be carnally minded is death; but to be spiritually minded is life and peace." The witnesses note the issue of the two minds — death or life and peace. Gill notes the phrase includes the best part of corrupt man — the mind, understanding, will, affections, thoughts; it may be rendered "the wisdom of the flesh," called so to distinguish it from the wisdom from above; the wisest part of man is but carnal. The witnesses note Paul declaring the issue — to be carnally minded is death, but to be spiritually minded is life and peace; the carnal mind ending in death (spiritual and eternal), the spiritual mind in life and peace; the two minds issuing in the two opposite ends; the fruit of each bent. The witnesses note carnal minding is death. The witnesses note spiritual minding is life and peace.

The witnesses note the two minds issuing in death or in life and peace — the fruit of the carnal and the spiritual bent.

So the witnesses make the verse the issue of the two minds. The lesson the expositors draw is that the carnal mind ends in death, the spiritual mind in life and peace — the two minds issuing in the two opposite ends. The witnesses note the carnal mind's issue: "to be carnally minded is death" — Gill notes the carnal mind (the wisdom of the flesh, including the best part of corrupt man — the mind, will, affections) issues in death; the carnal minding ending in death; the spiritual and eternal death; the fruit of the flesh. The expositors observe the spiritual mind's issue: "but to be spiritually minded is life and peace" — the spiritual mind issues in life and peace; the life (spiritual and eternal) and the peace (with God and in the conscience); the fruit of the Spirit; the blessed issue. The witnesses press the two ends: the two minds issue in the two opposite ends — death and life, condemnation and peace; the carnal to death, the spiritual to life; the ends determined by the bent; the fruit of each. The expositors draw the lesson on the carnal mind as death: the carnal mind is death — it is spiritual death now (alienation from God), and issues in eternal death; the death in the carnal mind; the bent toward death; "to be carnally minded is death." The witnesses observe the spiritual mind as life and peace: the spiritual mind is life and peace — spiritual life now (communion with God), and the prospect of eternal life, with the peace of reconciliation and a quiet conscience; the life and peace; the blessed state; the fruit of the Spirit's rule. The expositors note the wisdom of the flesh that Gill marks: even the wisest part of corrupt man — the "wisdom of the flesh" — is carnal, ending in death; the natural wisdom no help; the highest of the flesh still death; the wisdom of the flesh distinguished from the wisdom from above. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the carnal minding as death, the spiritual minding as life and peace, and the fruit of each bent. The verse thus records the two minds issuing in death or in life and peace — teaching that the carnal mind ends in death, the spiritual mind in life and peace.
7:
"Because the carnal mind is enmity against God: for it is not subject to the law of God, neither indeed can be." The witnesses note the carnal mind's enmity against God. Gill notes the carnal mind is not only an enemy, but enmity itself against God — against his being (it wishes he were not), his perfections, his purposes, his providences; against his law and his gospel. The witnesses note Paul giving the reason the carnal mind is death — it is enmity against God, not subject to his law, nor able to be; the deep, radical opposition of the unrenewed nature to God, and its utter inability to submit to his law; the depth of human depravity exposed. The witnesses note the carnal mind enmity. The witnesses note not subject, nor can be.

The witnesses note the carnal mind as enmity against God — not subject to his law, nor able to be; the depth of depravity.

So the witnesses make the verse the carnal mind's enmity against God. The lesson the expositors draw is the deep, radical opposition of the unrenewed nature to God, and its utter inability to submit to his law. The witnesses note the enmity: "the carnal mind is enmity against God" — Gill notes the carnal mind is not only an enemy, but enmity itself against God — against his being, perfections, purposes, providences, law, and gospel; the enmity; the radical opposition; the carnal mind hostile to God. The expositors observe the depth — enmity itself: the carnal mind is "enmity" — not merely at enmity, but enmity itself; the very nature opposed to God; the deep, ingrained hostility; the radical opposition; the enmity in the nature, not just the acts. The witnesses press the not-subjection: "for it is not subject to the law of God" — the carnal mind is not subject to God's law; it will not submit, will not obey, resists the law; the insubordination; the rebellion against the law; the will not subject. The expositors draw the lesson on the inability: "neither indeed can be" — the carnal mind not only is not, but cannot be, subject to God's law; the utter inability; the cannot; the depravity so deep that the unrenewed nature cannot submit; the total inability; "the natural man... cannot know them" (1 Corinthians 2:14). The witnesses observe the depth of depravity exposed: the carnal mind's enmity and inability expose the depth of human depravity — the unrenewed nature radically opposed to God, unable of itself to submit; the depravity; the inability; the need of regeneration; the corruption deep. The expositors note the necessity of the new nature: since the carnal mind cannot be subject to God, the sinner needs a new nature (the Spirit's work) to love and obey God; the necessity of regeneration; the new heart needed; the Spirit's renewal; the inability driving to grace. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the carnal mind enmity, the not subject nor can be, and the depth of depravity. The verse thus records the carnal mind as enmity against God — teaching the deep, radical opposition of the unrenewed nature to God and its utter inability to submit to his law.
8:
"So then they that are in the flesh cannot please God." The witnesses note that the unregenerate cannot please God. Barnes notes "they that are in the flesh" are unrenewed sinners, following supremely the desires of the flesh, not led by the Spirit; while they are thus in the flesh, they cannot please God. Gill notes they that are in the flesh are the same as those after it. The witnesses note Paul concluding that those in the flesh — the unregenerate, governed by corrupt nature — cannot please God; for, being enmity against God and unable to submit to his law (v.7), nothing they do can please him; the impossibility of pleasing God in the unrenewed state; the necessity of the new nature for any acceptable service. The witnesses note in the flesh. The witnesses note cannot please God.

The witnesses note the unregenerate cannot please God — being enmity against him and unable to submit; the necessity of the new nature.

So the witnesses make the verse the impossibility of pleasing God in the flesh. The lesson the expositors draw is that the unregenerate, being enmity against God and unable to submit, cannot please him — the necessity of the new nature for acceptable service. The witnesses note the class: "they that are in the flesh" — Barnes notes these are unrenewed sinners, following supremely the desires of the flesh, not led by the Spirit; Gill notes the same as those after the flesh; the unregenerate; those in the flesh; the carnal. The expositors observe the conclusion: "cannot please God" — those in the flesh cannot please God; the impossibility; the cannot; the unregenerate unable to please him; the conclusion from v.7. The witnesses press the reason: being enmity against God and unable to submit to his law (v.7), the unregenerate cannot please God — for nothing done in enmity, without faith, from a carnal heart, can please him; the reason in the enmity and inability; the carnal works unpleasing; "without faith it is impossible to please him" (Hebrews 11:6). The expositors draw the lesson on the impossibility: the unregenerate cannot please God — not merely do not, but cannot; for their best works, springing from a carnal, God-opposed heart, without faith, cannot please him; the impossibility; the cannot please; the works of the flesh rejected. The witnesses observe the necessity of the new nature: since those in the flesh cannot please God, the sinner needs the new nature (the Spirit) to render any acceptable service; the necessity of regeneration; the new heart for acceptable works; the Spirit needed; the new nature the requisite. The expositors note the comfort by contrast: the believer, not in the flesh but in the Spirit (v.9), can please God — his works, springing from faith and the Spirit, accepted; the contrast; the believer's service accepted; the comfort of the new state; the pleasing God by the Spirit. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the in the flesh, the cannot please God, and the necessity of the new nature. The verse thus records the unregenerate cannot please God — teaching the impossibility of pleasing God in the flesh, and the necessity of the new nature for acceptable service.
9:
"But ye are not in the flesh, but in the Spirit, if so be that the Spirit of God dwell in you. Now if any man have not the Spirit of Christ, he is none of his." The witnesses note the indwelling Spirit the mark of belonging to Christ. Barnes notes "ye" who are Christians are the opposite of those described — not under the full influence of corrupt desires, but spiritually minded, under the direction of the Holy Spirit. Gill notes they are not in a state of unregeneracy but of grace. The witnesses note Paul declaring that believers are not in the flesh but in the Spirit, if the Spirit of God dwells in them — and that anyone who has not the Spirit of Christ is none of his; the indwelling Spirit the decisive mark of belonging to Christ, the test of the true believer, without whom none is Christ's. The witnesses note the Spirit dwelling. The witnesses note none of his without the Spirit.

The witnesses note the indwelling Spirit the mark of belonging to Christ — without whom none is his; the decisive test.

So the witnesses make the verse the indwelling Spirit the mark of belonging to Christ. The lesson the expositors draw is that the indwelling Spirit is the decisive mark of the true believer, without whom none is Christ's. The witnesses note the new state: "ye are not in the flesh, but in the Spirit" — Barnes notes believers are the opposite of those described (v.8) — not under the corrupt desires, but spiritually minded, under the Spirit; Gill notes the state of grace, not unregeneracy; the new state; the in the Spirit; the believer's condition. The expositors observe the condition: "if so be that the Spirit of God dwell in you" — the new state is real if the Spirit of God dwells in them; the indwelling Spirit; the test of the state; the Spirit's residence; the condition. The witnesses press the decisive mark: "if any man have not the Spirit of Christ, he is none of his" — anyone who has not the Spirit of Christ is none of his; the indwelling Spirit the decisive mark; without the Spirit, no belonging to Christ; the test; the Spirit the seal of belonging. The expositors draw the lesson on the necessity of the Spirit: the Spirit of Christ is necessary to belong to Christ — without the indwelling Spirit, none is his, however he may profess; the Spirit the requisite; the indwelling the mark; the necessity of the Spirit; "if any man have not the Spirit of Christ, he is none of his." The witnesses observe the test of the true believer: the indwelling Spirit is the test — not profession, not outward religion, but the Spirit dwelling and working; the test of reality; the Spirit the evidence; the mark of the true; the searching test. The expositors note the Spirit called both of God and of Christ: the same Spirit is called "the Spirit of God" and "the Spirit of Christ" — the one Spirit proceeding from and given by both; the Spirit of the Father and the Son; the deity of the Spirit and of Christ implied; the one Spirit. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the Spirit dwelling, the none of his without the Spirit, and the decisive test. The verse thus records the indwelling Spirit the mark of belonging to Christ — teaching that the indwelling Spirit is the decisive mark of the true believer, without whom none is Christ's.
10:
"And if Christ be in you, the body is dead because of sin; but the Spirit is life because of righteousness." The witnesses note Christ in the believer, the body dead yet the Spirit life. Gill notes Christ is in his people not as in the world generally, but formed in their hearts by the Spirit in regeneration, dwelling in them as his own, manifesting himself to them. The witnesses note Paul declaring that if Christ is in the believer, the body is dead because of sin (still mortal, appointed to die for sin), but the Spirit (the renewed spirit, or the Holy Spirit) is life because of righteousness (the imputed righteousness giving life); the indwelling Christ, the mortal body, and the living spirit; death reigning in the body but life in the spirit through righteousness. The witnesses note Christ in you. The witnesses note the body dead, the Spirit life.

The witnesses note Christ in the believer — the body still mortal because of sin, but the spirit alive because of imputed righteousness.

So the witnesses make the verse Christ in the believer, the body dead, the Spirit life. The lesson the expositors draw is that, with Christ indwelling, the body remains mortal because of sin, but the spirit is alive because of the imputed righteousness. The witnesses note the indwelling: "if Christ be in you" — Gill notes Christ is in his people, formed in their hearts by the Spirit in regeneration, dwelling as his own; Christ indwelling; the union; the Christ in you; the inward presence. The expositors observe the body dead: "the body is dead because of sin" — the body is dead (mortal, appointed to die) because of sin; the body still subject to death; the mortality remaining; the body dead because of sin; the consequence of sin on the body. The witnesses press the spirit alive: "but the Spirit is life because of righteousness" — the spirit (the renewed human spirit, or, some hold, the Holy Spirit) is life because of righteousness; the spirit alive; the life because of the imputed righteousness; the living spirit; the life by righteousness. The expositors weigh "the Spirit": the witnesses note "the Spirit is life" may mean the believer's renewed spirit is alive (made alive in regeneration), or the Holy Spirit is life (the source of life) in him; the chief sense — the believer's spirit is alive (with spiritual, eternal life) because of the imputed righteousness; (some take it of the Holy Spirit as the life-giver); the spirit alive by righteousness. The expositors draw the lesson on the body and the spirit: in the believer, the body remains mortal (dying because of sin), but the spirit is alive (because of the righteousness of Christ imputed, giving spiritual and eternal life); the body and the spirit; the death and the life; the mortal body and the living spirit; the two states in the believer. The witnesses observe the "because of righteousness": the spirit is life "because of righteousness" — the imputed righteousness of Christ the ground of the spirit's life; the righteousness giving life; the life grounded in the righteousness; the imputed righteousness the source. The expositors note the hope of the body (v.11): though the body is dead because of sin, it shall be quickened (v.11) — the resurrection to come; the body's death not final; the mortal body to be raised; the hope of the body; the resurrection prospect. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks Christ in you, the body dead, the spirit life, and the righteousness the ground. The verse thus records Christ in the believer, the body dead, the spirit life — teaching that the body remains mortal because of sin, but the spirit is alive because of the imputed righteousness.
11:
"But if the Spirit of him that raised up Jesus from the dead dwell in you, he that raised up Christ from the dead shall also quicken your mortal bodies by his Spirit that dwelleth in you." The witnesses note the resurrection of the body by the indwelling Spirit. Gill notes this refers not to the continued work of sanctification but to the future quickening of the mortal body — the resurrection; for it is the mortal bodies, not the souls, that are said to be quickened. The witnesses note Paul declaring that if the Spirit of the God who raised Jesus dwells in the believer, God will also quicken (raise) his mortal body by that indwelling Spirit; the resurrection of the body the certain consequence of the indwelling Spirit, the same power that raised Christ raising the believer; the hope of the body grounded in the resurrection of Christ and the indwelling Spirit. The witnesses note the Spirit that raised Jesus. The witnesses note quicken mortal bodies.

The witnesses note the resurrection of the body by the indwelling Spirit — the power that raised Christ raising the believer.

So the witnesses make the verse the resurrection of the body by the indwelling Spirit. The lesson the expositors draw is that the resurrection of the body is the certain consequence of the indwelling Spirit, the same power that raised Christ raising the believer. The witnesses note the condition: "if the Spirit of him that raised up Jesus from the dead dwell in you" — if the Spirit of the God who raised Jesus dwells in the believer; the indwelling Spirit; the Spirit of the God of the resurrection; the condition. The expositors observe the promise: "he that raised up Christ from the dead shall also quicken your mortal bodies by his Spirit that dwelleth in you" — God, who raised Christ, will also quicken (raise) the believer's mortal body by the indwelling Spirit; the resurrection promised; the mortal body quickened; the raising. The witnesses press Gill's note that this is the resurrection, not sanctification: Gill notes this refers not to the continued work of sanctification, but to the future quickening of the mortal body — the resurrection; for it is the mortal bodies, not the souls, that are quickened; the bodily resurrection; the body raised. The expositors draw the lesson on the resurrection by the same power: the believer's body shall be raised by the same power that raised Christ — the indwelling Spirit the pledge and the agent; the resurrection by the same power; the power that raised Christ raising the believer; the certain resurrection; "Christ the firstfruits; afterward they that are Christ's" (1 Corinthians 15:23). The witnesses observe the indwelling Spirit the pledge: the indwelling Spirit is the pledge of the resurrection — for the Spirit who dwells in the believer is the same who raised Christ, and will quicken the body; the Spirit the earnest; the indwelling the guarantee; the pledge of the resurrection; the Spirit securing the hope. The expositors note the hope of the body: though the body is dead because of sin (v.10), it shall be quickened — the hope of the body; the mortal raised; the resurrection the answer to the body's death; the redemption of the body (v.23). The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the Spirit that raised Jesus, the quicken mortal bodies, and the resurrection by the indwelling Spirit. The verse thus records the resurrection of the body by the indwelling Spirit — teaching that the resurrection is the certain consequence of the indwelling Spirit, the same power that raised Christ raising the believer.
12:
"Therefore, brethren, we are debtors, not to the flesh, to live after the flesh." The witnesses note that believers are debtors, not to the flesh. Chrysostom notes that, having shown the great reward of the spiritual life (Christ dwelling, the body quickened), Paul fitly introduces an exhortation — yet not merely "we ought not to live after the flesh," but in a more striking way, "we are debtors"; saying "we are debtors not to the flesh" indicates we are debtors to the Spirit. The witnesses note Paul drawing the practical conclusion — we are debtors, not to the flesh, to live after it; the believer owes nothing to the flesh (which only brings death), but owes all to the Spirit (the giver of life); the obligation of grace, the debt of love to the Spirit, not the flesh. The witnesses note debtors not to the flesh. The witnesses note the obligation of grace.

The witnesses note believers debtors, not to the flesh — owing nothing to the flesh but all to the Spirit; the obligation of grace.

So the witnesses make the verse the believer's debt, not to the flesh. The lesson the expositors draw is that the believer owes nothing to the flesh, but owes all to the Spirit — the obligation of grace. The witnesses note the exhortation: "we are debtors, not to the flesh, to live after the flesh" — Chrysostom notes Paul, having shown the great reward of the spiritual life, introduces the exhortation; we are debtors, not to the flesh; the obligation; the debt; the practical conclusion. The expositors observe Chrysostom's note on the striking form: Paul does not merely say "we ought not to live after the flesh," but more strikingly, "we are debtors"; and saying "not to the flesh" indicates we are debtors to the Spirit; the debt to the Spirit implied; the obligation owned; the striking form. The witnesses press the no-debt to the flesh: the believer owes nothing to the flesh — for the flesh has done him no good, only brought death (v.13); no obligation to the flesh; the flesh deserving no service; the debt repudiated. The expositors draw the lesson on the obligation of grace: the believer is a debtor — but to the Spirit, the giver of life, not to the flesh; the obligation of grace; the debt of love to the Spirit; the believer owing all to grace; the obligation to live by the Spirit. The witnesses observe the implied debt to the Spirit: though Paul says "debtors not to the flesh," he implies the debt to the Spirit — the believer owes all to the Spirit who gave him life, dwells in him, will raise him; the debt to the Spirit; the obligation of gratitude; the service owed to the giver of life; the debt of grace. The expositors note the motive for holiness: the believer's debt is the motive for holiness — owing nothing to the flesh, all to the Spirit, he should live after the Spirit, not the flesh; the motive; the obligation moving to holiness; the debt of grace constraining; the gratitude that obeys. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the debtors not to the flesh, the obligation of grace, and the debt to the Spirit. The verse thus records believers debtors, not to the flesh — teaching that the believer owes nothing to the flesh but all to the Spirit, the obligation of grace.
13:
"For if ye live after the flesh, ye shall die: but if ye through the Spirit do mortify the deeds of the body, ye shall live." The witnesses note the two ways and their two ends, and the mortifying of sin. Gill notes that to live after the flesh, in a continued course of sinning, is contrary to the grace of God; though flesh remains in the believer, it has not the dominion, and the believer mortifies its deeds through the Spirit. The witnesses note Paul setting the alternatives — living after the flesh ends in death, but mortifying the deeds of the body through the Spirit ends in life; the necessity and the means of mortification; sin to be put to death not by mere resolution but through the Spirit, the believer's life evidenced and maintained in the killing of sin. The witnesses note live after the flesh, die. The witnesses note mortify through the Spirit, live.

The witnesses note the two ends — living after the flesh is death, mortifying sin through the Spirit is life; the necessity of mortification.

So the witnesses make the verse the two ways and the mortifying of sin. The lesson the expositors draw is the necessity and the means of mortification — sin put to death through the Spirit, the believer's life evidenced in the killing of sin. The witnesses note the way of death: "if ye live after the flesh, ye shall die" — Gill notes to live after the flesh, in a continued course of sinning, is contrary to the grace of God; the living after the flesh ending in death; the way of death; the warning; the carnal life fatal. The expositors observe the way of life: "if ye through the Spirit do mortify the deeds of the body, ye shall live" — to mortify (put to death) the deeds of the body through the Spirit ends in life; the mortifying; the killing of sin; the way of life; the Spirit-wrought death of sin. The witnesses press the means — through the Spirit: the mortifying is "through the Spirit" — not by mere resolution or human strength, but by the Spirit's power; the means; the Spirit the agent; the killing of sin by the Spirit; "by the Spirit." The expositors draw the lesson on the necessity of mortification: the believer must mortify the deeds of the body — the killing of sin necessary, evidence of life; the necessity; the duty of mortification; the putting to death of sin; "mortify therefore your members" (Colossians 3:5); the killing of sin essential. The witnesses observe the believer's part and the Spirit's: the believer mortifies ("ye... do mortify"), yet through the Spirit (the Spirit's power); the believer's act and the Spirit's power; the cooperation; the duty and the grace; the Spirit working in the believer's working. The expositors note the connection of mortification and life: "ye shall live" — the mortifying of sin issues in (and evidences) life; the killing of sin the path of life; the life maintained and evidenced in the war on sin; the mortification and the life; the holiness the way of life. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the living after the flesh as death, the mortifying through the Spirit as life, and the necessity of mortification. The verse thus records the two ways and the mortifying of sin — teaching the necessity and the means of mortification, sin put to death through the Spirit, the way of life.
14:
"For as many as are led by the Spirit of God, they are the sons of God." The witnesses note the leading of the Spirit the mark of sonship. Gill notes the Spirit leads them — from sin, from a dependence on their own righteousness, to Christ, in the paths of faith and truth; the leading supposes life and some strength, though much weakness, and is a display of efficacious grace, always for their good. The witnesses note Paul declaring that as many as are led by the Spirit of God are the sons of God; the Spirit's leading the mark of the sons, the gentle, efficacious guidance of the Spirit (from sin to Christ, in the way of holiness) the evidence of the adoption; the led the sons. The witnesses note led by the Spirit. The witnesses note the sons of God.

The witnesses note the Spirit's leading the mark of sonship — the gentle, efficacious guidance the evidence of adoption.

So the witnesses make the verse the Spirit's leading the mark of sonship. The lesson the expositors draw is that the Spirit's leading is the mark of the sons of God, the evidence of the adoption. The witnesses note the leading: "as many as are led by the Spirit of God" — Gill notes the Spirit leads them from sin, from self-righteousness, to Christ, in the paths of faith and truth; the leading; the Spirit's guidance; the led; the gentle leading. The expositors observe the gentleness and power: the leading supposes life and some strength (though much weakness), and is a display of efficacious grace, always for their good; the gentle leading (as a child taught to go); the efficacious grace; the powerful yet tender guidance; the leading for good. The witnesses press the mark of sonship: "they are the sons of God" — the led are the sons of God; the leading the mark; the Spirit's guidance the evidence of sonship; the sons known by the leading; the mark of the adoption. The expositors draw the lesson on the evidence of adoption: the Spirit's leading is the evidence of the adoption — those led by the Spirit (from sin to Christ, in holiness) are the sons of God; the evidence; the mark; the led the sons; the leading the proof of sonship. The witnesses observe the directions of the leading: the Spirit leads from sin (away from it), from self-righteousness (to Christ's), to Christ (for all), in the paths of faith, truth, and holiness; the leading from and to; the directions; the Spirit's guidance comprehensive; the led in the right way. The expositors note the comfort of the leading: the believer, conscious of the Spirit's leading (drawn from sin, to Christ, in holiness), has the evidence of his sonship; the comfort; the assurance from the leading; the mark of adoption; the led assured. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the led by the Spirit, the sons of God, and the leading the mark of sonship. The verse thus records the Spirit's leading the mark of sonship — teaching that as many as are led by the Spirit are the sons of God, the leading the evidence of the adoption.
15:
"For ye have not received the spirit of bondage again to fear; but ye have received the Spirit of adoption, whereby we cry, Abba, Father." The witnesses note the Spirit of adoption, not bondage. Gill notes the "spirit of bondage" is not the Holy Spirit (who is a free Spirit, a Spirit of liberty), but the bondage and fear under the law and conviction; the Spirit the believer receives is the Spirit of adoption, removing fear by discoveries of love. The witnesses note Paul declaring that believers have not received the spirit of bondage again to fear, but the Spirit of adoption, by whom they cry "Abba, Father"; the deliverance from the slavish fear of bondage and the gift of the filial spirit, the Spirit enabling the believer to approach God as Father with the cry of a child; the privilege of adoption. The witnesses note not bondage but adoption. The witnesses note Abba, Father.

The witnesses note the Spirit of adoption, not bondage — the filial spirit crying "Abba, Father," delivering from slavish fear.

So the witnesses make the verse the Spirit of adoption. The lesson the expositors draw is the deliverance from the slavish fear of bondage and the gift of the filial spirit, crying "Abba, Father." The witnesses note the negative: "ye have not received the spirit of bondage again to fear" — Gill notes the "spirit of bondage" is not the Holy Spirit (a free Spirit, a Spirit of liberty), but the bondage and fear under the law and conviction; the believer has not received this again; the bondage not the believer's state; the slavish fear removed. The expositors observe the positive: "but ye have received the Spirit of adoption" — the believer has received the Spirit of adoption — the Spirit who works the filial disposition, witnesses to the adoption, enables the child's approach; the Spirit of adoption; the filial Spirit; the gift of sonship. The witnesses press the cry "Abba, Father": "whereby we cry, Abba, Father" — by the Spirit of adoption the believer cries "Abba, Father" (Abba the Aramaic, Father the Greek — the cry of a child, perhaps embracing Jew and Gentile); the filial cry; the approach to God as Father; the child's address; the cry of adoption. The expositors draw the lesson on the deliverance from fear: the believer is delivered from the slavish fear of bondage (the dread of God as a judge under the law) and given the filial spirit (the love of God as a Father); the deliverance from fear; the slavish to the filial; the bondage to the adoption; "perfect love casteth out fear" (1 John 4:18). The witnesses observe the privilege of adoption: the believer is adopted — made a son, given the Spirit of adoption, enabled to cry "Abba, Father"; the privilege; the sonship; the access as a child; the adoption the great privilege; the Father's child. The expositors note the filial disposition: the Spirit of adoption works the filial disposition — the love, the trust, the reverence, the access of a child; the filial spirit; the heart of a son; the disposition of adoption; the child's spirit. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the not bondage but adoption, the Abba Father, and the deliverance from slavish fear. The verse thus records the Spirit of adoption, not bondage — teaching the deliverance from slavish fear and the gift of the filial spirit, crying "Abba, Father."
16:
"The Spirit itself beareth witness with our spirit, that we are the children of God:" The witnesses note the Spirit's witness to our adoption. Barnes notes the Holy Spirit is meant (it is of the Spirit that the apostle is treating, and it would be forced to say of the temper of adoption that it bore witness); the Spirit bears witness — testifies, gives evidence — with our spirit, to our minds, that we are children of God. The witnesses note Paul declaring that the Spirit himself bears witness with our spirit that we are the children of God; the double witness — the Holy Spirit and the believer's own spirit (the renewed conscience) together testifying to the adoption; the inward assurance of sonship wrought by the Spirit, the highest comfort of the believer. The witnesses note the Spirit bears witness. The witnesses note the double witness of sonship.

The witnesses note the Spirit bearing witness with our spirit that we are God's children — the inward assurance of sonship.

So the witnesses make the verse the Spirit's witness to our adoption. The lesson the expositors draw is the double witness — the Holy Spirit and the believer's own spirit — testifying to the adoption, the inward assurance of sonship. The witnesses note the witness: "the Spirit itself beareth witness" — Barnes notes the Holy Spirit is meant (the apostle treating of the Spirit; forced to say the temper of adoption bore witness); the Spirit testifies, gives evidence; the Spirit's witness; the divine testimony. The expositors observe the "with our spirit": "with our spirit, that we are the children of God" — the Spirit bears witness with our spirit (the believer's renewed spirit, the conscience); the double witness; the Spirit and our spirit together; the joint testimony; the witness to the mind. The witnesses press the double witness: the Holy Spirit and the believer's own spirit together testify to the adoption — the Spirit witnessing (by his work, his fruits, his enabling the filial cry), and the believer's spirit responding (conscious of the marks of grace); the double witness; the two testimonies; the joint assurance; the Spirit with our spirit. The expositors draw the lesson on the assurance of sonship: the Spirit's witness gives the believer the inward assurance that he is a child of God — the highest comfort, the sense of sonship wrought by the Spirit; the assurance; the inward witness; the comfort of the Spirit's testimony; the assured sonship. The witnesses observe how the Spirit witnesses: the Spirit witnesses by working the filial disposition (v.15), by his fruits (love, joy, peace), by enabling the cry "Abba, Father," by shining on the marks of grace; the manner of the witness; the Spirit's testimony through his work; the evidence of grace; the inward voice. The expositors note the caution against presumption and the comfort for the humble: the witness is the Spirit's, joined with the evidence of grace in the believer's spirit — not a bare impulse or presumption, but the Spirit's testimony confirmed by the fruits; the guard against presumption (the witness joined with the evidence); the comfort for the humble (the Spirit assuring the trembling child); the true assurance. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the Spirit bearing witness, the double witness, and the assurance of sonship. The verse thus records the Spirit bearing witness with our spirit that we are God's children — teaching the double witness of the Spirit and our spirit, the inward assurance of sonship.
17:
"And if children, then heirs; heirs of God, and joint-heirs with Christ; if so be that we suffer with him, that we may be also glorified together." The witnesses note the believer's heirship and the path of suffering to glory. Gill notes children, whether natural or adopted, are heirs to their parents; the believers, being children of God, are heirs — and according to the dignity of the inheritance, heirs of God himself and joint-heirs with Christ. The witnesses note Paul declaring that if children, then heirs — heirs of God and joint-heirs with Christ — if we suffer with him that we may be glorified together; the surpassing dignity of the heirship (God himself the inheritance, Christ the co-heir), and the path to the glory through suffering with Christ; the suffering and the glory joined. The witnesses note heirs of God. The witnesses note suffer with him, glorified together.

The witnesses note the believer heir of God and joint-heir with Christ — the path to glory through suffering with him.

So the witnesses make the verse the heirship and the path of suffering to glory. The lesson the expositors draw is the surpassing dignity of the heirship and the path to glory through suffering with Christ. The witnesses note the heirship: "if children, then heirs" — Gill notes children (natural or adopted) are heirs; the believers, being children of God, are heirs; the heirship flowing from the sonship; the children the heirs; the inheritance theirs. The expositors observe the dignity: "heirs of God, and joint-heirs with Christ" — the believers are heirs of God himself (God the inheritance, the portion) and joint-heirs with Christ (sharing his inheritance); the surpassing dignity; God the inheritance; Christ the co-heir; the exalted heirship. The witnesses press the surpassing inheritance: "heirs of God" — not merely heirs of created blessings, but of God himself — God the portion, the inheritance, the all; the highest inheritance; God himself the heritage; "the LORD is my portion" (Lamentations 3:24); the surpassing heritage. The expositors draw the lesson on joint-heirs with Christ: "joint-heirs with Christ" — the believers share Christ's inheritance, co-heirs with him, sharing his glory, his kingdom, his all; the joint-heirship; the union with Christ in the inheritance; the sharing of his glory; the co-heirs. The witnesses observe the path of suffering: "if so be that we suffer with him, that we may be also glorified together" — the path to the glory is through suffering with Christ; the suffering and the glory joined; the fellowship of his sufferings and the sharing of his glory; the cross before the crown; "if we suffer, we shall also reign with him" (2 Timothy 2:12). The expositors note the connection of suffering and glory: the suffering with Christ is the path to being glorified with him — not the meritorious cause, but the appointed way; the suffering preceding the glory; the cross the way to the crown; the fellowship in suffering and in glory; the path appointed. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the heirs of God, the joint-heirs with Christ, and the suffering with him to glory. The verse thus records the believer heir of God and joint-heir with Christ — teaching the surpassing dignity of the heirship and the path to glory through suffering with Christ.
18:
"For I reckon that the sufferings of this present time are not worthy to be compared with the glory which shall be revealed in us." The witnesses note the incomparable glory outweighing present sufferings. Gill notes "this present time" may mean the then-present suffering age, or the present time of life; the saints suffered much, yet grace and strength answerable to their trials were given. The witnesses note Paul reckoning that the sufferings of this present time are not worthy to be compared with the glory to be revealed in believers; the present afflictions, however great, infinitely outweighed by the coming glory; the calculation of faith that weighs the light, momentary suffering against the eternal weight of glory. The witnesses note the present sufferings. The witnesses note the glory to be revealed.

The witnesses note the present sufferings not worthy to be compared with the coming glory — the calculation of faith.

So the witnesses make the verse the incomparable glory outweighing the present sufferings. The lesson the expositors draw is the calculation of faith that weighs the light, momentary suffering against the eternal weight of glory. The witnesses note the reckoning: "I reckon" — Paul reckons (calculates, judges); the calculation of faith; the weighing; the considered judgment; the reckoning of the apostle. The expositors observe the present sufferings: "the sufferings of this present time" — Gill notes "this present time" the suffering age or the present life; the afflictions of the saints; the present sufferings; the trials of the time; the sufferings real. The witnesses press the incomparable glory: "are not worthy to be compared with the glory which shall be revealed in us" — the present sufferings are not worthy to be compared with the coming glory; the glory infinitely outweighing the suffering; the no-comparison; the glory surpassing; the incomparable weight. The expositors draw the lesson on the calculation of faith: faith weighs the light, momentary suffering against the eternal weight of glory, and reckons them not worthy of comparison; the calculation; "our light affliction, which is but for a moment, worketh for us a far more exceeding and eternal weight of glory" (2 Corinthians 4:17); the weighing of faith; the glory outweighing. The witnesses observe the glory revealed "in us": the glory shall be revealed "in us" — not merely to us, but in us; the believers themselves glorified; the glory wrought in them; the saints made glorious; the glory revealed in the heirs. The expositors note the comfort under suffering: the reckoning is the comfort under suffering — the present afflictions, however heavy, are nothing to the coming glory; the comfort; the suffering bearable in view of the glory; the encouragement; the glory sustaining. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the present sufferings, the glory to be revealed, and the calculation of faith. The verse thus records the present sufferings not worthy to be compared with the coming glory — teaching the calculation of faith that weighs the light suffering against the eternal weight of glory.
19:
"For the earnest expectation of the creature waiteth for the manifestation of the sons of God." The witnesses note the creation's earnest expectation. The expositors divide on "the creature/creation": Gill takes it (here and v.20-22) chiefly of the Gentile world, longing and groaning for deliverance and the influx of converts; Barnes and most take it of the natural, irrational creation, personified as eagerly waiting for the redemption. The witnesses note Paul declaring that the earnest expectation of the creation waits for the manifestation of the sons of God; the whole creation, subjected to the curse, longing with outstretched neck for the day when the sons of God shall be revealed in glory and the creation itself delivered; the groaning world awaiting the redemption. The witnesses note the earnest expectation. The witnesses note the manifestation of the sons.

The witnesses note the creation's earnest expectation, waiting for the manifestation of the sons of God — the groaning world awaiting redemption.

So the witnesses make the verse the creation's earnest expectation. The lesson the expositors draw is the creation, subjected to the curse, longing for the day when the sons of God shall be revealed and the creation delivered. The witnesses note the earnest expectation: "the earnest expectation of the creature" — the creation earnestly expects (the word a picture of one watching with outstretched neck); the eager waiting; the longing; the expectation; the creation's yearning. The expositors observe the divergence on "the creature": Gill takes it (here and v.20-22) chiefly of the Gentile world, longing for deliverance and the influx of converts; Barnes and most take it of the natural, irrational creation, personified as waiting for the redemption; the interpretive divergence; the creature the Gentiles or the natural creation. The witnesses present both readings: the dominant reading takes "the creation" as the whole irrational, natural creation — subjected to the curse (Genesis 3:17), groaning, waiting for deliverance; some (Gill) take it of the Gentile world; the witnesses note both, the chief sense the personified creation longing for the redemption. The expositors draw the lesson on the creation's longing: the creation, marred by the curse, longs for the day of restoration — when the sons of God shall be revealed and the creation itself delivered (v.21); the creation's longing; the groaning for restoration; the world awaiting redemption; the curse to be lifted. The witnesses observe the manifestation of the sons: "the manifestation of the sons of God" — the creation waits for the manifestation (the revealing in glory) of the sons of God; the sons now hidden, then revealed; the manifestation at the redemption; the glory of the sons displayed; the revealing of the heirs. The expositors note the link of the creation's destiny to the sons: the creation's deliverance is linked to the manifestation of the sons — when they are glorified, the creation shall be delivered; the destinies joined; the creation sharing the redemption; the world restored with the sons; the linked deliverance. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the earnest expectation, the manifestation of the sons, and the groaning world awaiting redemption. The verse thus records the creation's earnest expectation, waiting for the manifestation of the sons of God — teaching the creation, subjected to the curse, longing for the day of the sons' revealing and its own deliverance.
20:
"For the creature was made subject to vanity, not willingly, but by reason of him who hath subjected the same in hope," The witnesses note the creation subjected to vanity in hope. Gill, holding "the creature" to be the Gentiles, reads the vanity as their idolatry and empty state; Barnes and most read it of the natural creation subjected to vanity (frailty, decay, the curse) by God's appointment after the fall, "not willingly" (not by its own choice), yet "in hope" of deliverance. The witnesses note Paul declaring that the creation was made subject to vanity — not by its own will, but by him who subjected it — yet in hope; the creation brought under the curse and frailty by God's sentence (for man's sin), against its nature, but with the hope of future deliverance; the subjection and the hope. The witnesses note subjected to vanity. The witnesses note subjected in hope.

The witnesses note the creation subjected to vanity, not by its own will, but in hope — under the curse, yet with the hope of deliverance.

So the witnesses make the verse the creation subjected to vanity in hope. The lesson the expositors draw is the creation brought under the curse and frailty by God's sentence, against its nature, yet with the hope of deliverance. The witnesses note the subjection: "the creature was made subject to vanity" — the creation was subjected to vanity; Barnes and most read this of the natural creation subjected to frailty, decay, the curse (Genesis 3:17-18); Gill reads it of the Gentiles' empty, idolatrous state; the subjection to vanity; the bondage to frailty; the curse. The expositors observe the "not willingly": "not willingly, but by reason of him who hath subjected the same" — the creation was subjected not by its own will or choice, but by him (God) who subjected it; the subjection by God's sentence; not the creation's fault, but man's sin; the creation an innocent sufferer; the subjection from above. The witnesses press the cause — man's sin: the creation was subjected to vanity for man's sin — the ground cursed for Adam's sake (Genesis 3:17); the creation suffering for man; the curse on the world for sin; the subjection a consequence of the fall; the innocent creation bearing the effect. The expositors draw the lesson on the subjection by God: God subjected the creation to vanity — the sentence of the Judge after the fall; the divine subjection; the curse by God's appointment; the creation under the sentence; the subjection ordered by God. The witnesses observe the "in hope": "in hope" — the creation was subjected in hope (of deliverance, v.21); the subjection not final, but with hope; the hope of restoration; the curse not the last word; the hope in the subjection. The expositors note the comfort of the hope: even the creation's subjection was in hope — pointing to the coming deliverance; the comfort; the curse temporary; the hope of restoration; the creation's bondage to end. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the subjected to vanity, the not willingly, and the subjected in hope. The verse thus records the creation subjected to vanity in hope — teaching the creation brought under the curse by God's sentence for man's sin, yet with the hope of deliverance.
21:
"Because the creature itself also shall be delivered from the bondage of corruption into the glorious liberty of the children of God." The witnesses note the creation's coming deliverance. Gill, on his Gentile reading, sees the deliverance of the Gentiles from idolatry into gospel liberty; Barnes and most see the natural creation delivered from the bondage of corruption (decay, death, the curse) into a share in the liberty and glory of the children of God at the redemption. The witnesses note Paul declaring that the creation itself shall be delivered from the bondage of corruption into the glorious liberty of the children of God; the curse to be lifted, the creation freed from decay and death, sharing in the glory and liberty of the redeemed; the renewal of creation, the new heavens and new earth. The witnesses note delivered from bondage. The witnesses note the glorious liberty.

The witnesses note the creation delivered from the bondage of corruption into the glorious liberty of God's children — the renewal of creation.

So the witnesses make the verse the creation's coming deliverance. The lesson the expositors draw is the lifting of the curse — the creation freed from corruption, sharing the liberty and glory of the redeemed. The witnesses note the deliverance: "the creature itself also shall be delivered from the bondage of corruption" — the creation shall be delivered from the bondage of corruption; Barnes and most see the natural creation freed from decay, death, the curse; Gill (his Gentile reading) sees the Gentiles freed from idolatry; the deliverance; the bondage lifted; the corruption ended. The expositors observe the bondage of corruption: "the bondage of corruption" — the creation's present bondage to corruption (decay, death, frailty, the curse); the bondage; the corruption; the present groaning state; the world under decay. The witnesses press the glorious liberty: "into the glorious liberty of the children of God" — the creation shall be brought into the glorious liberty of the children of God — sharing in the freedom and glory of the redeemed; the liberty; the glory; the creation made free and glorious; the share in the children's liberty. The expositors draw the lesson on the renewal of creation: the creation shall be renewed — freed from the curse, made new, glorious; the new heavens and new earth (2 Peter 3:13; Revelation 21:1); the renewal of creation; the world restored; the curse lifted; the creation glorified. The witnesses observe the creation's share in the redemption: the creation shares in the redemption — its deliverance linked to the manifestation of the sons (v.19); the world redeemed with the sons; the creation partaking of the glory; the redemption cosmic; the whole creation renewed. The expositors note the comfort of the renewal: the believer is comforted by the prospect of the renewed creation — the curse lifted, the world made new, the glory shared; the comfort; the hope of the new creation; the restoration of all things (Acts 3:21); the glorious prospect. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the delivered from bondage, the glorious liberty, and the renewal of creation. The verse thus records the creation delivered from the bondage of corruption into the glorious liberty of God's children — teaching the lifting of the curse and the renewal of creation, sharing the glory of the redeemed.
22:
"For we know that the whole creation groaneth and travaileth in pain together until now." The witnesses note the whole creation groaning in travail. Barnes takes "the whole creation" in the natural, usual sense — the creation groaning, as in birth-pangs, longing for the deliverance. Gill reads the groaning of the Gentile world. The witnesses note Paul declaring that the whole creation groans and travails in pain together until now; the universal groaning of the creation, likened to the pangs of childbirth — the pain not of death but of a coming birth, the travail issuing in the new creation; the world's groaning a sign of the deliverance to come. The witnesses note the creation groaning. The witnesses note the travail in pain.

The witnesses note the whole creation groaning and travailing in pain — the pangs not of death but of a coming birth, the new creation.

So the witnesses make the verse the whole creation groaning in travail. The lesson the expositors draw is the universal groaning of the creation, likened to birth-pangs — the travail issuing in the new creation. The witnesses note the groaning: "the whole creation groaneth" — Barnes takes "the whole creation" in the natural sense, groaning; Gill reads the Gentile world; the creation groaning; the universal groan; the world's distress; the groaning of the creation. The expositors observe the travail: "and travaileth in pain together until now" — the creation travails in pain together — the word a picture of the pangs of childbirth; the travail; the birth-pangs; the pain of labour; the groaning as in travail. The witnesses press the meaning of the travail: the creation's pain is not the pang of death, but of birth — the travail that issues in a new creation; the pangs of labour, not of dissolution; the suffering that precedes the birth; the new creation coming; the travail toward the deliverance. The expositors draw the lesson on the hope in the groaning: the groaning and travail are a sign of hope — for the travail issues in birth; the pain points to the coming deliverance; the groaning not despair but expectation; the travail toward the new creation; the hope in the pain. The witnesses observe the universality: "the whole creation... together" — the whole creation groans together, in one universal travail; the universal groaning; the whole world in pain; the creation united in the longing; the universal travail. The expositors note the "until now": "until now" — the creation has groaned from the fall until now, and groans still; the long travail; the groaning continuing; the pain ongoing; the travail until the redemption. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the creation groaning, the travail in pain, and the pangs of the coming birth. The verse thus records the whole creation groaning and travailing in pain — teaching the universal groaning of the creation, the pangs not of death but of a coming birth, the new creation.
23:
"And not only they, but ourselves also, which have the firstfruits of the Spirit, even we ourselves groan within ourselves, waiting for the adoption, to wit, the redemption of our body." The witnesses note believers also groaning, waiting for the redemption of the body. Barnes notes "the firstfruits of the Spirit" (using the harvest figure) means the first portion, the present graces and influences of the Spirit, the earnest of the full harvest to come. The witnesses note Paul declaring that not only the creation, but believers also, having the firstfruits of the Spirit, groan within themselves, waiting for the adoption — the redemption of the body; the believer's inward groaning amid present imperfection, longing for the consummation of the adoption in the resurrection and glorification of the body; the firstfruits now, the full harvest then. The witnesses note the firstfruits of the Spirit. The witnesses note the redemption of the body.

The witnesses note believers groaning, waiting for the redemption of the body — the firstfruits of the Spirit now, the full adoption then.

So the witnesses make the verse believers groaning for the redemption of the body. The lesson the expositors draw is the believer's inward groaning amid present imperfection, longing for the consummation of the adoption in the resurrection of the body. The witnesses note the firstfruits: "which have the firstfruits of the Spirit" — Barnes notes "the firstfruits of the Spirit" (the harvest figure) means the first portion, the present graces of the Spirit, the earnest of the full harvest; the firstfruits; the present possession; the earnest of more; the down-payment of glory. The expositors observe the groaning: "even we ourselves groan within ourselves" — believers also groan within themselves; the inward groaning; the longing amid imperfection; the groaning of the saints; the inward sigh; the present distress. The witnesses press the object of the longing: "waiting for the adoption, to wit, the redemption of our body" — believers wait for the adoption — namely, the redemption of the body; the full adoption (the public manifestation, v.19) consummated in the redemption (resurrection and glorification) of the body; the body to be redeemed; the adoption completed; the longing for the consummation. The expositors draw the lesson on the present and the future adoption: the believer is already adopted (v.15), yet waits for the full adoption — the redemption of the body; the present adoption and the future consummation; the firstfruits now, the harvest then; the already and the not-yet; the adoption to be completed. The witnesses observe the redemption of the body: the consummation is "the redemption of our body" — the resurrection and glorification of the body, freed from corruption, made like Christ's glorious body (Philippians 3:21); the body redeemed; the resurrection; the glorified body; the full redemption. The expositors note the groaning of the firstfruits: the believer, having the firstfruits, groans the more — for having tasted the Spirit, he longs for the fulness; the groaning of those who have the earnest; the firstfruits intensifying the longing; the taste increasing the hunger; the earnest stirring the hope. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the firstfruits of the Spirit, the redemption of the body, and the groaning for the consummation. The verse thus records believers groaning, waiting for the redemption of the body — teaching the believer's inward groaning amid present imperfection, longing for the consummation of the adoption in the resurrection of the body.
24:
"For we are saved by hope: but hope that is seen is not hope: for what a man seeth, why doth he yet hope for?" The witnesses note salvation in the mode of hope. Gill notes the saints were already saved in the purposes of God, the covenant, and the purchase of Christ, yet the full enjoyment is future, possessed now in hope. The witnesses note Paul declaring that we are saved by (or in) hope, for hope that is seen is not hope — what a man sees he does not hope for; the present salvation held in the mode of hope, the full enjoyment future and unseen, the believer saved in hope, waiting for what he does not yet see; the nature of hope as reaching toward the unseen. The witnesses note saved by hope. The witnesses note hope reaches the unseen.

The witnesses note salvation held in the mode of hope — the full enjoyment future and unseen; hope reaches toward what is not yet seen.

So the witnesses make the verse salvation in the mode of hope. The lesson the expositors draw is that the present salvation is held in the mode of hope, the full enjoyment future and unseen. The witnesses note the saved by hope: "we are saved by hope" — Gill notes the saints were already saved in God's purposes, the covenant, and Christ's purchase, yet the full enjoyment is future, held in hope; saved by (or in) hope; the present salvation in the mode of hope; the salvation possessed in expectation. The expositors observe the nature of hope: "hope that is seen is not hope" — hope that is seen (already possessed, visible) is not hope; hope reaches toward the unseen, the future, the not-yet-possessed; the nature of hope; the reaching toward the unseen; the hope of the future. The witnesses press the reasoning: "for what a man seeth, why doth he yet hope for?" — what a man sees (already has) he does not hope for; hope is for the unseen, the future; the reasoning; the hope toward the not-yet; the object of hope unseen; the future the object. The expositors draw the lesson on the future and unseen salvation: the believer's full salvation is future and unseen — held in hope, not yet possessed in sight; the salvation in hope; the future enjoyment; the unseen glory; the hope reaching toward it; the not-yet of salvation. The witnesses observe the present and future salvation: the believer is already saved (in God's purpose, in Christ, in the firstfruits), yet waits for the full salvation (the redemption of the body, the glory); the already and the not-yet; the present possession and the future enjoyment; the salvation begun and to be consummated; the hope spanning the two. The expositors note the comfort and discipline of hope: the salvation in hope both comforts (the certain prospect) and disciplines (the waiting for the unseen); the comfort of the hope; the discipline of the waiting; the hope that sustains and trains; the hope's double work. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the saved by hope, the hope reaching the unseen, and the future salvation. The verse thus records salvation held in the mode of hope — teaching that the present salvation is held in hope, the full enjoyment future and unseen, hope reaching toward what is not yet seen.
25:
