"But if we hope for that we see not, then do we with patience wait for it." The witnesses note the patient waiting of hope. Barnes notes this effect exists everywhere: where there is a strong desire for an object and a corresponding expectation of obtaining it (which constitutes true hope), we can wait for it with patience. The witnesses note Paul declaring that if we hope for what we do not see, we wait for it with patience; the patient endurance that hope produces, the believer waiting with steadfast patience for the unseen glory; the patience of hope, the quiet, steadfast expectation that sustains the soul through the present groaning. The witnesses note hope for the unseen. The witnesses note patient waiting.

The witnesses note the patient waiting that hope produces — the believer waiting with steadfast patience for the unseen glory.

So the witnesses make the verse the patient waiting of hope. The lesson the expositors draw is the patient endurance that hope produces, the believer waiting with steadfast patience for the unseen glory. The witnesses note the hope for the unseen: "if we hope for that we see not" — the believer hopes for what he does not see (the future glory, the redemption of the body); the hope toward the unseen; the object future; the hope of the not-yet; the unseen glory. The expositors observe the patient waiting: "then do we with patience wait for it" — the believer waits for it with patience; the patient waiting; the steadfast expectation; the enduring hope; the patience of hope. The witnesses press Barnes's note on the nature of hope: where there is a strong desire and a corresponding expectation of obtaining (which constitutes true hope), we can wait with patience; the strong desire and the expectation; the true hope; the patient waiting flowing from the hope; the hope producing the patience. The expositors draw the lesson on the patience of hope: hope produces patience — the believer, expecting the unseen glory, waits with steadfast patience; the patience of hope; the quiet, enduring expectation; the patience sustaining the soul; the hope that waits. The witnesses observe the sustaining of the soul: the patient waiting of hope sustains the soul through the present groaning (v.23) — the believer, hoping for the glory, endures the present trials with patience; the sustaining; the hope upholding; the patience through the groaning; the soul sustained by hope. The expositors note the contrast with impatience: where there is desire without the corresponding expectation, there is impatience; but true hope (desire with expectation) produces patient waiting; the contrast; the hope that waits and the impatience that frets; the patience of the assured. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the hope for the unseen, the patient waiting, and the sustaining of the soul. The verse thus records the patient waiting that hope produces — teaching the patient endurance that hope works, the believer waiting with steadfast patience for the unseen glory.
26:
"Likewise the Spirit also helpeth our infirmities: for we know not what we should pray for as we ought: but the Spirit itself maketh intercession for us with groanings which cannot be uttered." The witnesses note the Spirit helping our infirmities in prayer. Gill notes the Spirit who dwells in us, leads us, witnesses to our adoption, also helps our infirmities — the people of God all have their infirmities, and weakness in prayer is one. The witnesses note Paul declaring that the Spirit helps our infirmities, for we know not what to pray for as we ought, but the Spirit himself makes intercession for us with groanings that cannot be uttered; the Spirit's inward help in prayer, supplying our ignorance and weakness, exciting and shaping the unutterable groanings of the praying heart; the intercession of the indwelling Spirit. The witnesses note the Spirit helps our infirmities. The witnesses note groanings unutterable.

The witnesses note the Spirit helping our infirmities in prayer — interceding with unutterable groanings, supplying our ignorance and weakness.

So the witnesses make the verse the Spirit's help in prayer. The lesson the expositors draw is the Spirit's inward help, supplying our ignorance and weakness, exciting and shaping the unutterable groanings of the praying heart. The witnesses note the help: "the Spirit also helpeth our infirmities" — Gill notes the Spirit who dwells in us, leads us, witnesses to our adoption, also helps our infirmities; the people of God have infirmities, weakness in prayer one; the Spirit's help; the infirmities helped; the inward aid. The expositors observe the infirmity in prayer: "for we know not what we should pray for as we ought" — we know not what to pray for as we ought; the ignorance in prayer; the weakness; the not knowing the right matter or manner; the infirmity of the praying soul. The witnesses press the intercession of the Spirit: "the Spirit itself maketh intercession for us with groanings which cannot be uttered" — the Spirit himself makes intercession for us with unutterable groanings; the Spirit's intercession; the groanings too deep for words; the Spirit shaping the prayer; the inward intercession. The expositors draw the lesson on the Spirit's inward work: the Spirit intercedes not apart from us, but in us — exciting, shaping, and bearing up the believer's prayers; the Spirit working in the praying heart; the inward intercession; the Spirit prompting the groanings; the help within. The witnesses observe the unutterable groanings: "groanings which cannot be uttered" — the Spirit excites groanings too deep for words; the unutterable longings; the wordless prayer; the groanings of the heart that words cannot express; the deep desires the Spirit stirs. The expositors note the comfort of the help: the believer, weak and ignorant in prayer, is helped by the Spirit — who supplies what is lacking, shapes the prayer, bears it up; the comfort; the help in weakness; the Spirit's aid; the prayer assisted; the weak prayer upheld. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the Spirit helping our infirmities, the unutterable groanings, and the Spirit's intercession. The verse thus records the Spirit helping our infirmities in prayer — teaching the Spirit's inward help, supplying our ignorance and weakness, exciting the unutterable groanings of the praying heart.
27:
"And he that searcheth the hearts knoweth what is the mind of the Spirit, because he maketh intercession for the saints according to the will of God." The witnesses note God knowing the mind of the interceding Spirit. Barnes notes "he that searcheth the hearts" is God — searching the heart his incommunicable attribute (Jeremiah 17:10); he knows the mind of the Spirit, the desires the Spirit excites in the heart. The witnesses note Paul declaring that God, who searches the hearts, knows the mind of the Spirit — for the Spirit makes intercession for the saints according to the will of God; the heart-searching God understanding the unutterable groanings the Spirit prompts, and those groanings being according to his will, the believer's prayers (Spirit-shaped) are sure to be heard. The witnesses note God searches the hearts. The witnesses note intercession according to God's will.

The witnesses note God knowing the mind of the interceding Spirit — the Spirit's intercession according to God's will, sure to be heard.

So the witnesses make the verse God knowing the mind of the interceding Spirit. The lesson the expositors draw is that the heart-searching God understands the Spirit's unutterable groanings, which, being according to his will, are sure to be heard. The witnesses note the heart-searcher: "he that searcheth the hearts" — Barnes notes this is God, searching the heart his incommunicable attribute (Jeremiah 17:10; 1 Chronicles 28:9); the heart-searching God; the omniscient; the one who knows the heart; the God who searches. The expositors observe the knowing of the Spirit's mind: "knoweth what is the mind of the Spirit" — God knows the mind of the Spirit — the desires the Spirit excites in the heart; the unutterable groanings (v.26) known to God; the Spirit's mind understood; the wordless prayer read by God; the groanings interpreted. The witnesses press the intercession according to God's will: "because he maketh intercession for the saints according to the will of God" — the Spirit makes intercession for the saints according to the will of God; the intercession conformed to God's will; the Spirit-shaped prayers in line with the divine will; the will of God the rule; the prayer according to God. The expositors draw the lesson on the certainty of the answer: because the Spirit's intercession is according to God's will, and God knows the Spirit's mind, the believer's Spirit-shaped prayers are sure to be heard; the certainty; the prayer according to God's will answered; the sure hearing; "if we ask any thing according to his will, he heareth us" (1 John 5:14); the answered prayer. The witnesses observe the comfort of the Spirit's interpreting: the believer's groanings, too deep for words (v.26), are known to God and shaped by the Spirit to God's will; the comfort; the inarticulate prayer understood; the Spirit bridging the weakness; the prayer heard though unutterable; the comfort of the interpreted groan. The expositors note the harmony of the divine Persons: the heart-searching Father knows the mind of the interceding Spirit, whose intercession is according to the Father's will; the harmony of the Persons; the Spirit and the Father in accord; the Trinitarian comfort; the prayer carried by the Spirit to the Father's will. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the God who searches the hearts, the intercession according to God's will, and the certainty of the answer. The verse thus records God knowing the mind of the interceding Spirit — teaching that the heart-searching God understands the Spirit's groanings, which, being according to his will, are sure to be heard.
28:
"And we know that all things work together for good to them that love God, to them who are the called according to his purpose." The witnesses note the great assurance that all things work for the good of God's people. Chrysostom notes Paul mooted this topic for those in danger — teaching them not to choose just what they may think best, but to leave it to God, who works all for good. The witnesses note Paul declaring the assurance that all things work together for good to them that love God, the called according to his purpose; the providence that overrules all things — even afflictions and sufferings — for the believer's good; the good defined by the calling and the love of God, the assurance grounded in God's purpose. The witnesses note all things work for good. The witnesses note the called according to his purpose.

The witnesses note all things working together for good to them that love God — the providence overruling all for the believer's good.

So the witnesses make the verse the assurance that all things work for good. The lesson the expositors draw is the providence that overrules all things, even afflictions, for the believer's good, grounded in God's purpose. The witnesses note the assurance: "we know that all things work together for good" — the believer knows that all things work together for good; the certain assurance; the "we know"; the confident knowledge; the assurance of providence. The expositors observe Chrysostom's note on the comfort to the suffering: Paul mooted this for those in danger — teaching them to leave it to God, who works all for good; the comfort to the afflicted; the assurance for the suffering; the providence overruling the trials; the good in all. The witnesses press the scope — all things: "all things work together for good" — all things, including afflictions, sufferings, even sins repented of, work together (in concert) for good; the all-embracing providence; the all things; the working together; the universal overruling; the good from all. The expositors draw the lesson on the providence overruling for good: God overrules all things — the pleasant and the painful, the prosperous and the adverse — for the believer's good; the providence; the overruling; the all things serving the good; "the LORD shall... work all things for good"; the providential governance. The witnesses observe the good defined: the "good" is the believer's true, spiritual, eternal good — conformity to Christ (v.29), final salvation, the glory; not always temporal ease, but the highest good; the good defined; the spiritual good; the eternal benefit; the true good. The expositors note the persons described: "to them that love God, to them who are the called according to his purpose" — the assurance is for those who love God, the called according to his purpose; the love of God the mark, the calling the ground; the called according to purpose; the persons defined by love and calling; the believer's double mark. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the all things working for good, the called according to his purpose, and the providence overruling all. The verse thus records all things working together for good to them that love God — teaching the providence that overrules all things for the believer's good, grounded in God's purpose.
29:
"For whom he did foreknow, he also did predestinate to be conformed to the image of his Son, that he might be the firstborn among many brethren." The witnesses note the foreknowledge and predestination to be conformed to Christ. Gill argues the "foreknowledge" here is not the bare prescience of all things future (for then all would be predestinated and glorified), but a special, distinguishing foreknowledge — God's foreknowing in the sense of foreloving, setting his love on a select people. The witnesses note Paul beginning the golden chain — whom God foreknew (foreloved), he predestinated to be conformed to the image of his Son, that Christ might be the firstborn among many brethren; the divine purpose to make the chosen like Christ, the end of predestination conformity to the Son; the foreknowledge as foreloving, the great debate. The witnesses note foreknowledge as foreloving. The witnesses note conformed to the image of Christ.

The witnesses note God foreknowing (foreloving) and predestinating his people to be conformed to Christ — the foreknowledge as electing love.

So the witnesses make the verse the foreknowledge and predestination to conformity to Christ. The lesson the expositors draw is the divine purpose to make the chosen like Christ, and the debated meaning of "foreknow." The witnesses note the foreknowledge: "whom he did foreknow" — Gill argues the foreknowledge here is not the bare prescience of all things future (for then all would be predestinated and glorified), but a special, distinguishing foreknowledge — God's foreknowing in the sense of foreloving, setting his love on a select people; the foreknowledge as electing love; the foreloved. The expositors present the debate on "foreknow": the Reformed read "foreknow" as foreloved, foreordained to love (not bare prescience) — the chosen people God set his love on; the Arminian read it as God's foresight of who would believe — foreknowing the faith, then predestinating; the great debate; the foreknowledge as love or as prescience. The witnesses give the Reformed argument: Gill argues that if "foreknow" meant bare prescience of all, then all (foreknown) would be predestinated, called, justified, glorified — which is not so; therefore the foreknowledge is special, distinguishing, a foreloving of the elect; the argument from the chain (all the foreknown glorified, v.30); the foreknowledge as electing love. The expositors note the predestination: "he also did predestinate to be conformed to the image of his Son" — the foreknown are predestinated to be conformed to the image of Christ; the predestination; the destiny to Christlikeness; the end conformity to the Son; the purpose of the predestining. The witnesses press the end — conformity to Christ: the end of predestination is conformity to the image of the Son — the chosen made like Christ, in holiness now and glory hereafter; the conformity; the Christlikeness; the goal of election; the image of the Son; the predestined likeness. The expositors observe the firstborn among many brethren: "that he might be the firstborn among many brethren" — Christ the firstborn among many brethren — the head, the pattern, the elder brother of the redeemed family; the firstborn; the preeminence; the brethren conformed to him; the family of God with Christ the head. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the foreknowledge as foreloving, the conformity to Christ, and the firstborn among many brethren. The verse thus records the foreknowledge and predestination to conformity to Christ — teaching the divine purpose to make the chosen like Christ, the foreknowledge as electing love.
30:
"Moreover whom he did predestinate, them he also called: and whom he called, them he also justified: and whom he justified, them he also glorified." The witnesses note the unbroken golden chain of salvation. Gill notes this predestination is of particular persons, the effect of divine grace, the source of all the other blessings, placed at the head and securing them all; the called, justified, and glorified all flowing from it. The witnesses note Paul completing the golden chain — whom God predestinated, he called; whom he called, justified; whom he justified, glorified; the unbroken chain of salvation, each link drawing the next, from the eternal predestination through the effectual calling and justification to the certain glorification; the security of the elect, none lost from the chain. The witnesses note the golden chain. The witnesses note the certain glorification.

The witnesses note the unbroken golden chain — predestined, called, justified, glorified; the security of the elect, none lost.

So the witnesses make the verse the golden chain of salvation. The lesson the expositors draw is the unbroken chain, each link drawing the next, securing the elect from predestination to glory. The witnesses note the chain: "whom he did predestinate, them he also called: and whom he called, them he also justified: and whom he justified, them he also glorified" — the chain of salvation; predestined, called, justified, glorified; the links; the golden chain; the order of salvation. The expositors observe Gill's note on the source: this predestination is of particular persons, the source of all the other blessings, placed at the head and securing them all; the predestination the fountain; the chain flowing from it; the blessings secured. The witnesses press the unbroken chain: each link draws the next — the predestined are all called, the called all justified, the justified all glorified; the unbroken chain; none lost between the links; the same "whom... them" throughout; the chain complete; the elect secured. The expositors draw the lesson on the security of the elect: the chain secures the elect — none predestined is lost, but all are called, justified, and glorified; the security; the perseverance; the elect kept; "of all which he hath given me I should lose nothing" (John 6:39); the unbroken salvation; the chain unbreakable. The witnesses observe the certain glorification: "them he also glorified" — the glorification spoken of as already done ("glorified," past tense) — so certain it is reckoned as accomplished; the certainty; the glorification sure; the past tense of certainty; the glory as good as done; the assured end. The expositors note the comfort of the chain: the golden chain is the believer's comfort — the whole of salvation, from eternity to glory, secured by God; the comfort; the assurance; the salvation in God's hand; the chain held by God; the believer secure in the chain. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the golden chain, the certain glorification, and the security of the elect. The verse thus records the unbroken golden chain — predestined, called, justified, glorified — teaching the security of the elect, each link drawing the next from predestination to glory.
31:
"What shall we then say to these things? If God be for us, who can be against us?" The witnesses note the triumphant challenge — God for us. Gill notes the question may look to the afflictions (shall we murmur? no) or to the blessings just mentioned (foreknowledge, predestination, calling, justification, glorification) — what can be said to these? a complete set of blessings, wanting nothing. The witnesses note Paul drawing the triumphant conclusion — what shall we say to these things? If God be for us, who can be against us; the believer, having God himself on his side (the God of the golden chain), need fear no adversary, for none can prevail against the one whom the Almighty defends; the security of the believer in the favour of God. The witnesses note God for us. The witnesses note who against us.

The witnesses note the triumphant challenge — if God be for us, who can be against us; the believer secure in the favour of the Almighty.

So the witnesses make the verse the triumphant challenge of God for us. The lesson the expositors draw is that the believer, having God himself on his side, need fear no adversary, for none can prevail against the one whom the Almighty defends. The witnesses note the question: "what shall we then say to these things?" — Gill notes the question may look to the afflictions or the blessings (the golden chain); what shall we say to these? the triumphant question; the response to the doctrine; the conclusion drawn. The expositors observe the challenge: "if God be for us, who can be against us?" — if God is for us, who can be against us; the triumphant challenge; the believer with God on his side; the defiance of all adversaries; the security in God. The witnesses press God for us: "if God be for us" — God is for the believer — the God of the golden chain (v.29-30), who predestined, called, justified, and will glorify; God on the believer's side; the Almighty for him; the favour of God; the great "if" (a settled fact, not a doubt). The expositors draw the lesson on the security in God's favour: with God for him, the believer is secure — none can prevail against the one whom the Almighty defends; the security; the favour of God the shield; no adversary able to prevail; "the LORD is on my side; I will not fear" (Psalm 118:6); the believer safe. The witnesses observe the "who can be against us": "who can be against us?" — though many are against the believer (the world, Satan, sin, death), none can prevail — for God is greater than all; the adversaries real but powerless; the none-can-prevail; the enemies overcome; the foes unable. The expositors note the triumphant tone: the verse opens the triumphant conclusion of the chapter (v.31-39) — the believer's security in the love and favour of God; the triumph; the assurance; the confident challenge; the soaring conclusion; the victory declared. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the God for us, the who can be against us, and the security in God's favour. The verse thus records the triumphant challenge — if God be for us, who can be against us — teaching the believer's security in the favour of the Almighty, against whom no adversary can prevail.
32:
"He that spared not his own Son, but delivered him up for us all, how shall he not with him also freely give us all things?" The witnesses note the argument from the greater gift to the lesser. Gill notes it is amazing that God should not spare "his own Son" — his proper Son, of the same nature, equal to him, the Son of his love, who never sinned — when he spared not the angels that sinned, nor the old world; he delivered him up for us all. The witnesses note Paul reasoning from the greatest gift — God, who spared not his own Son but delivered him up for us all, will surely with him freely give us all things; the "much more" argument from the cross — if God gave the greatest (his Son), he will not withhold the lesser (all things needful); the security grounded in the unsparing gift of the Son. The witnesses note spared not his own Son. The witnesses note freely give us all things.

The witnesses note the argument from the greater gift — God who spared not his Son will freely give us all things; security grounded in the cross.

So the witnesses make the verse the argument from the greatest gift. The lesson the expositors draw is the "much more" from the cross — if God gave the greatest (his Son), he will not withhold the lesser (all things needful). The witnesses note the unsparing gift: "he that spared not his own Son" — Gill notes it is amazing that God should not spare his own Son — his proper Son, of the same nature, equal to him, the Son of his love, sinless — when he spared not the sinning angels nor the old world; the unsparing of the Son; the greatest gift; the Son not spared; the amazing gift. The expositors observe the delivering up: "but delivered him up for us all" — God delivered up his Son for us all (to death, for the believers); the delivering up; the giving of the Son; the sacrifice; the Son given for us; the gift of the cross. The witnesses press the argument: "how shall he not with him also freely give us all things?" — if God gave his own Son (the greatest gift), how shall he not also freely give us all things (the lesser)? the "much more" argument; the greater includes the less; the argument from the cross; the all things assured by the gift of the Son. The expositors draw the lesson on the security from the greatest gift: the unsparing gift of the Son is the ground of the believer's security — God, having given the greatest, will not withhold the lesser; the security; the all things assured; the gift of the Son the pledge of all; "with him also freely give us all things"; the cross the guarantee. The expositors note the "all things" in Christ: the "all things" are given "with him" — in and through Christ; the all things flowing from the gift of the Son; the blessings in Christ; the all things with the Son; the gift comprehensive; "all things are yours... and ye are Christ's" (1 Corinthians 3:21-23). The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the spared not his own Son, the freely give us all things, and the security from the greatest gift. The verse thus records the argument from the greatest gift — God who spared not his Son will freely give us all things — teaching the believer's security grounded in the unsparing gift of the Son.
33:
"Who shall lay any thing to the charge of God's elect? It is God that justifieth." The witnesses note the challenge — none can charge the justified elect. Gill notes the elect of God are a select number whom he has loved and chosen in Christ before the foundation of the world; though chargeable in themselves with sin, none can lay anything to their charge, for it is God that justifies. The witnesses note Paul issuing the challenge — who shall lay anything to the charge of God's elect? It is God that justifies; the believer secure against all accusation, for the highest Judge himself has acquitted them — no charge can stand against those whom God has justified; the security of the justified against every accuser. The witnesses note none can charge the elect. The witnesses note God justifies.

The witnesses note the challenge — none can charge God's elect, for it is God that justifies; the security against all accusation.

So the witnesses make the verse the challenge against all accusation. The lesson the expositors draw is that no charge can stand against those whom God has justified — the believer secure against every accuser. The witnesses note the challenge: "who shall lay any thing to the charge of God's elect?" — Paul challenges any to lay a charge against God's elect; Gill notes the elect a select number loved and chosen in Christ before the foundation of the world; the challenge; the daring of any accuser; the elect addressed. The expositors observe the answer: "It is God that justifieth" — none can charge the elect, for it is God that justifies; the highest Judge has acquitted them; the justification by God; the acquittal; the answer to the challenge. The witnesses press the security against accusation: since God himself — the highest Judge — has justified the elect, no charge can stand; the accuser is overruled by the Judge; the security against all accusation; the charge dismissed by God's acquittal; the believer cleared by the highest court. The expositors draw the lesson on the acquittal by God: it is God that justifies — the very one before whom the charge would be laid has acquitted; the Judge himself the justifier; the charge answered by the Judge's acquittal; "if God justify, who can condemn?"; the acquittal final. The witnesses observe that the elect are chargeable in themselves: Gill notes the elect are chargeable in themselves with sin (Adam's sin, their own sins); yet none can lay it to their charge — for God has justified them, the sin imputed to Christ; the sins real but not charged; the guilt covered; the charge void by the justification. The expositors note the comfort against the accuser: the believer, accused by Satan, conscience, or the law, is secure — God has justified; the accuser silenced; the comfort against the charge; "the accuser of our brethren is cast down" (Revelation 12:10); the believer cleared. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the challenge against the charge, God justifies, and the security against accusation. The verse thus records the challenge — none can charge God's elect, for it is God that justifies — teaching the believer's security against all accusation, acquitted by the highest Judge.
34:
"Who is he that condemneth? It is Christ that died, yea rather, that is risen again, who is even at the right hand of God, who also maketh intercession for us." The witnesses note the challenge — none can condemn, for Christ died, rose, and intercedes. Gill notes none of God's elect can be condemned, for they are loved with an everlasting love, chosen, in Christ where there is no condemnation, justified by him. The witnesses note Paul issuing the second challenge — who condemns? It is Christ that died, rather is risen, who is at God's right hand, who intercedes for us; the fourfold ground of security — Christ's death (the satisfaction), resurrection (the acceptance), exaltation (the authority), and intercession (the continual advocacy) — against which no condemnation can stand. The witnesses note none can condemn. The witnesses note Christ died, risen, interceding.

The witnesses note the challenge — none can condemn, for Christ died, rose, reigns, and intercedes; the fourfold ground of security.

So the witnesses make the verse the challenge against condemnation. The lesson the expositors draw is the fourfold ground of security — Christ's death, resurrection, exaltation, and intercession — against which no condemnation can stand. The witnesses note the challenge: "who is he that condemneth?" — Paul challenges any to condemn; Gill notes none of God's elect can be condemned, being in Christ where there is no condemnation; the challenge; the daring of any to condemn; the security. The expositors observe the fourfold ground: "It is Christ that died, yea rather, that is risen again, who is even at the right hand of God, who also maketh intercession for us" — the fourfold ground — Christ's death, resurrection, exaltation, intercession; the four pillars; the ground of security; the answer to condemnation. The witnesses press each ground: Christ "died" (the satisfaction for sin, the penalty borne); "is risen again" (the proof of the accepted satisfaction, the justification, Romans 4:25); "is even at the right hand of God" (the exaltation, the authority, the place of power); "maketh intercession for us" (the continual advocacy, pleading his finished work); the four grounds; the death, resurrection, exaltation, intercession. The expositors draw the lesson on the security against condemnation: against the fourfold ground, no condemnation can stand — the death answered the penalty, the resurrection proved the acquittal, the exaltation gives the authority, the intercession secures the continuance; the security; no condemnation; the fourfold defense; "he is able... to save them to the uttermost... seeing he ever liveth to make intercession" (Hebrews 7:25). The witnesses observe the intercession: "who also maketh intercession for us" — the living Christ intercedes — pleading his death, presenting his merits, securing the believer's standing; the intercession; the advocacy; the continual pleading; "we have an advocate with the Father" (1 John 2:1); the intercession the crown of the security. The expositors note the "yea rather": "yea rather, that is risen again" — the resurrection emphasized above the death — for the risen, living, interceding Christ is the believer's continual security; the living Christ; the resurrection the more; the living advocate; the security in the living Lord. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the none can condemn, the Christ died, risen, interceding, and the fourfold ground. The verse thus records the challenge — none can condemn, for Christ died, rose, reigns, and intercedes — teaching the fourfold ground of security against all condemnation.
35:
"Who shall separate us from the love of Christ? shall tribulation, or distress, or persecution, or famine, or nakedness, or peril, or sword?" The witnesses note the challenge — nothing can separate from the love of Christ. Gill notes "the love of Christ" is not the saints' love to Christ, but his love to them; the apostle's design is to strengthen the faith and comfort the souls of God's people under their trials. The witnesses note Paul issuing the challenge — who shall separate us from the love of Christ? — and naming the severest trials (tribulation, distress, persecution, famine, nakedness, peril, sword); the believer's union with Christ's love unbreakable by any affliction, the very sufferings that seem to threaten it unable to sever it; the security of the believer in Christ's love. The witnesses note the love of Christ. The witnesses note the trials that cannot separate.

The witnesses note the challenge — nothing can separate us from the love of Christ; the severest trials unable to sever the union.

So the witnesses make the verse the challenge against separation. The lesson the expositors draw is that the believer's union with Christ's love is unbreakable by any affliction. The witnesses note the challenge: "who shall separate us from the love of Christ?" — Paul challenges any to separate the believer from Christ's love; Gill notes "the love of Christ" is his love to them, not theirs to him; the challenge; the union threatened; the love of Christ to us. The expositors observe the trials named: "shall tribulation, or distress, or persecution, or famine, or nakedness, or peril, or sword?" — the severest trials named — tribulation, distress, persecution, famine, nakedness, peril, sword; the catalogue of afflictions; the worst sufferings; the trials that seem to threaten. The witnesses press the love of Christ to us: the love is Christ's love to the believer — his everlasting, unchanging love; Gill notes the design to strengthen and comfort under trials; the love of Christ to us the bond; the love unchanging; the love that holds. The expositors draw the lesson on the unbreakable union: no affliction can separate the believer from Christ's love — the very sufferings that seem to threaten it are unable to sever it; the union unbreakable; the love unsevered; the trials powerless to separate; the bond secure. The witnesses observe the severity of the trials: the trials named are the severest — affliction, want, danger, death; yet none can separate; the worst sufferings; the extreme trials; the catalogue of the dreadful; the trials that test but cannot sever. The expositors note the comfort under trials: the believer, under the severest trials, is comforted — none can separate him from Christ's love; the comfort; the assurance under affliction; the love that holds through all; the security in the love; the bond that the trial cannot break. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the love of Christ, the trials named, and the unbreakable union. The verse thus records the challenge — nothing can separate us from the love of Christ — teaching the unbreakable union of the believer with Christ's love, unsevered by the severest trials.
36:
"As it is written, For thy sake we are killed all the day long; we are accounted as sheep for the slaughter." The witnesses note the Scripture confirming the saints' sufferings. Gill notes this is cited from Psalm 44, the meaning being that for the sake of God and his worship the Old Testament saints were frequently put to death or exposed to persecution, as New Testament saints have been for Christ and his gospel. Barnes notes Paul quotes it not as originally referring to Christians, but as aptly descriptive of their condition. The witnesses note Paul citing the psalm — for God's sake we are killed all the day long, accounted as sheep for the slaughter; the appointed lot of the people of God to suffer, the constant exposure to death for the truth; the sufferings real and continual, yet (the next verse) not separating from the love. The witnesses note killed all the day long. The witnesses note sheep for the slaughter.

The witnesses note the Scripture confirming the saints' constant sufferings — killed all the day long, as sheep for the slaughter.

So the witnesses make the verse the Scripture confirming the saints' sufferings. The lesson the expositors draw is the appointed lot of the people of God to suffer, the constant exposure to death for the truth. The witnesses note the citation: "as it is written, For thy sake we are killed all the day long" — Gill notes this is cited from Psalm 44:22, the saints frequently put to death for God's sake; Barnes notes Paul quotes it as aptly descriptive of the Christians' condition; the Scripture; the citation; the psalm applied. The expositors observe the constant suffering: "killed all the day long" — the saints killed all the day long — the constant, continual exposure to death; the perpetual suffering; the daily dying; the ongoing peril; "I die daily" (1 Corinthians 15:31). The witnesses press the sheep for the slaughter: "we are accounted as sheep for the slaughter" — the saints accounted as sheep for the slaughter — counted as victims, helpless, appointed to die; the sheep for slaughter; the helpless victims; the appointed sacrifice; the lot of the persecuted. The expositors draw the lesson on the appointed lot of suffering: the people of God are appointed to suffer — for God's sake, for the truth, for Christ; the suffering the lot; the cross the portion; the persecution appointed; "all that will live godly... shall suffer persecution" (2 Timothy 3:12); the appointed afflictions. The witnesses observe the reason — "for thy sake": the suffering is "for thy sake" — for God's sake, for his worship, for Christ and the gospel; the suffering for God; the cause the truth; the persecution for righteousness; the suffering for the Lord. The expositors note the realness of the sufferings: the sufferings are real, constant, severe (killed all the day, as sheep for slaughter) — not imagined or light; yet (v.37) they do not separate from the love; the real sufferings; the severe trials; the constant peril; the realness owned. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the killed all the day long, the sheep for the slaughter, and the appointed lot of suffering. The verse thus records the Scripture confirming the saints' sufferings — teaching the appointed lot of the people of God to suffer, the constant exposure to death for the truth.
37:
"Nay, in all these things we are more than conquerors through him that loved us." The witnesses note the believer more than conqueror in all trials. Gill notes "in all these things" — afflictions, distresses, persecutions, famine, nakedness, sword — the believer is not separated, but conquers. Barnes notes that in the very midst of them, while enduring them, the believer is able to triumph — "more than" conquerors. The witnesses note Paul declaring that in all these things believers are more than conquerors through him that loved them; not merely surviving or escaping the trials, but triumphing over them, made the gainers by them, through the love of Christ; the trials turned to victory, the believer more than victorious through the loving Saviour. The witnesses note more than conquerors. The witnesses note through him that loved us.

The witnesses note the believer more than conqueror in all trials — triumphing over them, made the gainer, through the love of Christ.

So the witnesses make the verse the believer more than conqueror. The lesson the expositors draw is that in all trials the believer not merely survives but triumphs, made the gainer, through the love of Christ. The witnesses note the "in all these things": "in all these things" — Gill notes the afflictions, distresses, persecutions, famine, nakedness, sword (v.35-36); Barnes notes in the very midst of them, while enduring them; the trials; the sufferings; the afflictions in which the believer conquers. The expositors observe the "more than conquerors": "we are more than conquerors" — the believer is more than a conqueror — not merely surviving or escaping, but triumphing, made the gainer; Barnes notes the triumph in the midst of the trials; the more-than-victory; the surpassing conquest; the triumph beyond mere survival. The witnesses press the meaning of "more than conquerors": the believer is more than a conqueror — for he not only overcomes the trials, but is made better by them (the trials working patience, experience, hope, v.3-4, and conformity to Christ); the trials turned to gain; the victory that profits; the more-than-conquest; the gain from the loss. The expositors draw the lesson on the triumph through love: the believer is more than conqueror "through him that loved us" — through Christ's love, the trials are turned to victory; the triumph through Christ; the love the power; the victory by the loving Saviour; "thanks be to God, which giveth us the victory through our Lord Jesus Christ" (1 Corinthians 15:57). The witnesses observe the trials made servants: the trials, far from separating, are made the believer's servants — working his good, his patience, his conformity to Christ, his glory; the trials serving; the afflictions turned to gain; the sufferings made servants; the more-than-conquest. The expositors note the ground in Christ's love: the victory is "through him that loved us" — grounded in Christ's love, not the believer's strength; the love the source; the triumph by the loving Christ; the victory through the love; the ground in the Saviour. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the more than conquerors, the through him that loved us, and the triumph in all trials. The verse thus records the believer more than conqueror in all trials — teaching that in all things the believer triumphs, made the gainer, through the love of Christ.
38:
"For I am persuaded, that neither death, nor life, nor angels, nor principalities, nor powers, nor things present, nor things to come," The witnesses note Paul's full persuasion that nothing can separate. Gill notes these words express the strong persuasion and full assurance of faith — not conjectural but certain, not from special revelation but founded on the nature of God's love, its security in Christ, eternal predestination, and the unalterable covenant. The witnesses note Paul beginning his triumphant catalogue — persuaded that neither death, nor life, nor angels, nor principalities, nor powers, nor things present, nor things to come, can separate us; the full assurance that ranges over all conceivable powers and circumstances, finding none able to sever the believer from God's love; the certainty of the persuasion grounded in the unchanging love of God. The witnesses note the full persuasion. The witnesses note neither death nor life.

The witnesses note Paul's full persuasion that neither death, life, angels, powers, nor time can separate us — grounded in God's unchanging love.

So the witnesses make the verse Paul's full persuasion. The lesson the expositors draw is the full assurance that ranges over all powers and circumstances, finding none able to sever the believer from God's love. The witnesses note the persuasion: "I am persuaded" — Gill notes this expresses the strong persuasion and full assurance of faith — not conjectural but certain, founded on the nature of God's love, its security in Christ, eternal predestination, and the covenant; the full assurance; the persuasion; the certainty; the confident conviction. The expositors observe the catalogue: "neither death, nor life, nor angels, nor principalities, nor powers, nor things present, nor things to come" — the catalogue of powers and circumstances — death, life, angels, principalities, powers, things present, things to come; the comprehensive list; the all-embracing survey; the powers named. The witnesses press the comprehensiveness: the catalogue ranges over all — death (the last enemy), life (with its allurements and trials), angels (good or evil), principalities and powers (the spiritual hosts), things present and to come (all time); the comprehensive survey; the all conceivable; nothing omitted; the full range. The expositors draw the lesson on the certainty of the persuasion: Paul's persuasion is certain — not a hope or a guess, but a full assurance, grounded in the unchanging love of God; the certainty; the full assurance; the grounded conviction; "I know whom I have believed" (2 Timothy 1:12); the sure persuasion. The witnesses observe the ground — God's love: the persuasion rests on the nature of God's love, its security in Christ, the eternal predestination, the covenant; the ground in God; the love unchanging; the security in Christ; the foundation; the love the ground of the assurance. The expositors note the triumphant tone: the catalogue mounts to the triumphant climax (v.39) — nothing can separate; the soaring assurance; the triumph; the confidence; the climax of the chapter; the victory declared over all powers. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the full persuasion, the neither death nor life, and the comprehensive assurance. The verse thus records Paul's full persuasion that no power or circumstance can separate — teaching the full assurance grounded in the unchanging love of God.
39:
"Nor height, nor depth, nor any other creature, shall be able to separate us from the love of God, which is in Christ Jesus our Lord." The witnesses note the triumphant climax — nothing can separate from the love of God in Christ. Gill notes "nor height, nor depth" — neither heaven, earth, nor hell, nor any inhabitant of either; no height of honour and prosperity, nor depth of adversity; no power or policy in men or devils. The witnesses note Paul completing the triumphant declaration — nor height, nor depth, nor any other creature, shall separate us from the love of God in Christ Jesus our Lord; the final, all-comprehending assurance, that nothing in all creation can sever the believer from God's love, which is secured in Christ; the unbreakable bond of the love of God, the soaring conclusion of the chapter. The witnesses note nothing can separate. The witnesses note the love of God in Christ.

The witnesses note the climax — nothing in all creation can separate us from the love of God in Christ; the unbreakable bond.

So the witnesses make the verse the triumphant climax. The lesson the expositors draw is the final, all-comprehending assurance that nothing in all creation can sever the believer from God's love, secured in Christ. The witnesses note the completion of the catalogue: "nor height, nor depth, nor any other creature" — Gill notes "height, depth" — heaven, earth, hell, any inhabitant, any height of prosperity or depth of adversity, any power of men or devils; "any other creature" — anything else in all creation; the catalogue completed; the all-comprehending; nothing omitted. The expositors observe the climax: "shall be able to separate us from the love of God, which is in Christ Jesus our Lord" — nothing shall be able to separate the believer from the love of God in Christ; the climax; the final assurance; the unbreakable bond; the love secured. The witnesses press the comprehensiveness — any other creature: "nor any other creature" — anything else in all creation; the all-comprehending; nothing in heaven, earth, or hell; no created thing; the universe surveyed and found powerless; the complete assurance. The expositors draw the lesson on the unbreakable bond: nothing in all creation can sever the believer from God's love — the bond unbreakable; the love secure; the union eternal; the assurance complete; "I give unto them eternal life; and they shall never perish" (John 10:28); the bond that holds. The witnesses observe the love secured in Christ: "the love of God, which is in Christ Jesus our Lord" — the love of God is in Christ — secured, conveyed, guaranteed in him; the love in Christ; the union with Christ the security; the love through the Mediator; the bond in Christ; the love grounded in him. The expositors note the soaring conclusion: the verse crowns the chapter (and the great argument, Romans 3-8) — the believer's complete, eternal security in the love of God; the soaring conclusion; the triumph; the assurance complete; the summit of the epistle; the believer eternally secure. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the nothing can separate, the love of God in Christ, and the unbreakable bond. The verse thus records the triumphant climax — nothing in all creation can separate us from the love of God in Christ — teaching the final, all-comprehending assurance of the believer's eternal security in God's love, secured in Christ.
